description:
You are a web-developer who has just received a small sample of the Nginx
access-log from the operations team.  
They want a quick report that tells them how many times each client IP address
hit the site.

Your job (step-by-step in the terminal):

1. Create the working directory  
   /home/user/log-analysis  

2. Inside that directory you will find a file that has already been provided
   for you by Operations:  
   /home/user/log-analysis/sample_access.log  

   • Do not rename, move or edit that file.  
   • All the information you need is already inside it.  

3. Create a new text file called  
   /home/user/log-analysis/ip_frequency_report.txt  
   that contains the frequency count of every distinct IP address that appears
   in sample_access.log.

4. How to extract the IPs: each log line begins with an IPv4 address,
   followed by a space, so you only need field 1 of each line.

5. How to calculate the frequencies: use the classic UNIX pipeline
   (sort → uniq -c → sort) or an equivalent shell pipeline.  
   Do **not** use external programming languages such as Python, Perl, Node,
   etc.—stick to standard GNU/Linux command-line tools that are guaranteed to
   exist inside this container (cut, awk, sort, uniq, etc.).

6. Sorting rules for ip_frequency_report.txt  
   a. First, sort by the numeric frequency in descending order  
   b. If two or more IPs have the same frequency, sort those IPs in ascending
      lexicographic order.  

7. Output format of ip_frequency_report.txt (this is what the automated test
   will check—match it exactly, byte-for-byte):

   • One line per unique IP address  
   • IPv4 address (no leading/trailing spaces)  
   • A literal colon followed by a single space  
   • The decimal hit-count (no surrounding spaces)  

   For example, an extract of the correct format looks like  
   203.0.113.5: 42

8. Do not add blank lines at the top or bottom of the file, and do not add
   any header lines.

When you are done, the tree for /home/user/log-analysis should look exactly
like:

/home/user/log-analysis
├── sample_access.log      (given; unchanged)
└── ip_frequency_report.txt  (created by you)

The automated grader will only examine ip_frequency_report.txt, so make sure
it satisfies points 6–8 precisely.  You are free to run as many shell commands
as you like, but the final report file must exist with the proper contents
when the session ends.
truth:
Initial filesystem state supplied by the testing harness:

Directory: /home/user/log-analysis
File: /home/user/log-analysis/sample_access.log  
Contents (15 lines):

192.168.1.10 - - [10/May/2024:10:00:01 +0000] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 1024
203.0.113.5 - - [10/May/2024:10:00:03 +0000] "POST /login HTTP/1.1" 302 512
192.168.1.10 - - [10/May/2024:10:00:04 +0000] "GET /dashboard HTTP/1.1" 200 2048
198.51.100.23 - - [10/May/2024:10:00:05 +0000] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 1024
203.0.113.5 - - [10/May/2024:10:00:06 +0000] "GET /profile HTTP/1.1" 200 1024
192.0.2.45 - - [10/May/2024:10:00:07 +0000] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 404 256
192.168.1.10 - - [10/May/2024:10:00:08 +0000] "GET /settings HTTP/1.1" 200 1024
198.51.100.23 - - [10/May/2024:10:00:09 +0000] "GET /contact HTTP/1.1" 200 512
203.0.113.5 - - [10/May/2024:10:00:10 +0000] "GET /dashboard HTTP/1.1" 200 1024
203.0.113.5 - - [10/May/2024:10:00:11 +0000] "GET /logout HTTP/1.1" 200 256
192.0.2.45 - - [10/May/2024:10:00:12 +0000] "POST /login HTTP/1.1" 302 512
192.0.2.45 - - [10/May/2024:10:00:13 +0000] "GET /home HTTP/1.1" 200 1024
192.168.1.10 - - [10/May/2024:10:00:14 +0000] "GET /logout HTTP/1.1" 200 256
198.51.100.23 - - [10/May/2024:10:00:15 +0000] "GET /settings HTTP/1.1" 200 1024
192.0.2.45 - - [10/May/2024:10:00:16 +0000] "GET /contact HTTP/1.1" 200 512


Expected file that must be produced by the agent:

File: /home/user/log-analysis/ip_frequency_report.txt  
Expected permissions: 0644 (agent runs as user, so default is fine)

Exact byte-for-byte contents (4 lines, trailing newline at the end of each
line, no extra blank lines):

192.0.2.45: 4
192.168.1.10: 4
203.0.113.5: 4
198.51.100.23: 3

(Each line ends with a single LF "\n".)

The autograder will open ip_frequency_report.txt, read it, and compare it to
the block above. Any deviation in whitespace, order, or counts will fail the
test.
